papers.title: Predicting the hypervelocity star population in Gaia
papers.author_names: T. Marchetti O. Contigiani E. M. Rossi J. G. Albert A. G. A. Brown A. Sesana

abstract.text: Hypervelocity stars (HVSs) are amongst the fastest objects in our Milky Way. These stars are predicted to come from the Galactic center (GC) and travel along unbound orbits across the Galaxy. In the coming years, the ESA satellite Gaia will provide the most complete and accurate catalogue of the Milky Way, with full astrometric parameters for more than $1$ billion stars. In this paper, we present the expected sample size and properties (mass, magnitude, spatial, velocity distributions) of HVSs in the Gaia stellar catalogue. We build three Gaia mock catalogues of HVSs anchored to current observations, exploring different ejection mechanisms and GC stellar population properties. In all cases, we predict hundreds to thousands of HVSs with precise proper motion measurements within a few tens of kpc from us. For stars with a relative error in total proper motion below $10 \\\\%$, the mass range extends to ~$10 M_{\\\\odot}$ but peaks at ~$1$ $M_\\\\odot$. The majority of Gaia HVSs will therefore probe a different mass and distance range compared to the current non-Gaia sample. In addition, a subset of a few hundreds to a few thousands of HVSs with $M$ ~ $3$ $M_\\\\odot$ will be bright enough to have a precise measurement of the three-dimensional velocity from Gaia alone. Finally, we show that Gaia will provide more precise proper motion measurements for the current sample of HVS candidates. This will help identifying their birthplace narrowing down their ejection location, and confirming or rejecting their nature as HVSs. Overall, our forecasts are extremely encouraging in terms of quantity and quality of HVS data that can be exploited to constrain both the Milky Way potential and the GC properties.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper forecasts the expected sample size, mass distribution, and kinematic properties of hypervelocity stars (HVSs) in the Gaia catalogue by constructing three mock catalogues based on different ejection mechanisms (Hills, MBHB, and alternative models) and GC stellar populations. It predicts hundreds to thousands of HVSs with precise proper motions, peaking at ~1 M☉ and extending up to ~10 M☉, most of which will be at distances of tens of kpc with improved velocity measurements. A subset of ~3 M☉ HVSs will have sufficient brightness for full 3D velocity determination from Gaia alone, enabling better ejection origin identification and constraints on Galactic potential and central black hole properties.

source_fields.title: Predicting the hypervelocity star population in Gaia

source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Discussion: Gaia is expected to detect several hundred to several thousand hypervelocity stars (HVSs) with precise proper motions, primarily at heliocentric distances below 50 kpc, with a peak around 10 kpc, and will enable data mining techniques to distinguish them from the Galactic background without relying on radial velocity measurements. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel analysis of the sky distribution of hypervelocity stars, proposing that the observed clump in the Leo direction is not due to a single ejection mechanism but rather a combined signature of multiple processes, including the Hills mechanism, massive black hole binaries, and potential contributions from the Large Magellanic Cloud, thereby solving the long-standing problem of reconciling the observed non-isotropic distribution with theoretical predictions. The "Mbhb" Catalogue: This section details the simulation of hypervelocity star ejections from a massive black hole binary in the Galactic Center, comparing the orbital decay rates due to stellar ejections and gravitational wave emission to determine the binary's evolution and ejection dynamics. The "Hills" Catalogue: This section presents a physically motivated model for high-velocity stars (HVSs) using the Hills mechanism, where binary stars near the galactic center are ejected with velocities derived from a three-body interaction, resulting in a population of both unbound and bound HVSs, distinct from the earlier simplified model. Prospects For The Current Sample Of Hvss: Gaia's end-of-mission proper motion measurements are expected to significantly improve the accuracy of HVS trajectory determinations, enabling better constraints on their Galactic origin and helping to test alternative ejection models. THE "VESC" Mock CATALOGUE: A SIMPLE APPROACH: The paper proposes a synthetic population method, termed VESC, to model the astrometric properties of hypervelocity stars (HVSs) by assuming their total velocity equals the Galactic escape velocity at their position, and computes their proper motions under radial trajectories away from the Galactic center, enabling a forecast of Gaia's measurement precision without assuming specific ejection mechanisms.

abstract: Hypervelocity stars (HVSs) are amongst the fastest objects in our Milky Way. These stars are predicted to come from the Galactic center (GC) and travel along unbound orbits across the Galaxy.
In the coming years, the ESA satellite Gaia will provide the most complete and accurate catalogue of the Milky Way, with full astrometric parameters for more than $1$ billion stars.
For stars with a relative error in total proper motion below $10 \\\\%$, the mass range extends to ~$10 M_{\\\\odot}$ but peaks at ~$1$ $M_\\\\odot$.
title: Predicting the hypervelocity star population in Gaia

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper presents theoretical predictions and mock catalogs of hypervelocity stars in Gaia, not actual observational data
